title: Corrugated Metal Raised Planter
summary: The fastest raised bed in the catalog: two cedar-clad 2x4 leg frames joined by a wrap of corrugated roofing tin. Two steps, an afternoon hour, done.
description:
This is the raised bed for people who want to be planting by lunch. The wood part is just two end assemblies — 2x4 legs clad with short cedar picket boards — and the entire body of the planter is a piece of corrugated metal roofing screwed to them. The corrugations do the structural work a wall of lumber would normally do: that ribbed profile is why roofing panels can span rafters, and here it's why the sides don't need studs, rails, or a frame between the ends.

The listed panel is 26" x 36", but the design scales with the material: a 36"-wide panel makes a deeper bed, and longer panels make longer beds — the pickets and 2x4s don't care. Metal roofing is sold in standard widths at every farm store, usually cheaper per square foot than any wood you'd replace it with.

Two things the steps won't forgive you for skipping. First: cut metal edges are SHARP, and so are the corrugation ends. File or tape any edge that ends up near the soil line where hands will work, or hide the cut edges against the wood ends. Second: use actual roofing screws (the kind with bonded rubber washers) to attach the tin — plain screws through metal leave ragged holes that weep rust streaks down the panel after the first watering.

Line it with landscape fabric, fill, plant. Choose red-toned or treated 2x4s — the legs sit against damp soil and ground splash for their whole service life.

tools.note: Drives everything, including the roofing screws through the tin ribs.
The eight wood cuts. The metal panel is used as purchased — no metal cutting in the stock size.
Break the picket edges — this is a bed you'll lean over all season.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Red-toned or treated — the legs live against damp soil.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
Corrugated metal roofing, 26" x 36" panel
materials.note: A 36"-wide panel makes a deeper bed; longer panels make longer beds. Farm-store standard sizes.

materials.name: Roofing screws with washers, 1-1/4"
materials.note: The gasketed kind — they seal their own holes and stop rust streaks.

materials.name: Landscape fabric
materials.note: Liner. Soil goes in it, not against bare tin and cedar.

cutList.note: Three boards per end, screwed across the leg pairs.

steps.title: Build the two end assemblies
steps.body:
For each end, lay a pair of 20" legs parallel and screw three 18" picket boards across them with the 1-1/2" screws and glue — a little ladder, pickets on the outside face.

Build the second one on top of the first so they match exactly. These two frames are the entire skeleton; if they agree with each other, the planter comes out square on its own.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared

steps.title: Wrap the tin and fill
steps.body:
Bend the corrugated panel around between the two end assemblies and screw it to the legs with the gasketed roofing screws — drive through the ribs into the 2x4s, snug enough to seat the washer without crushing the corrugation. Space the screws evenly; the tin carries the soil load out to the legs through those fasteners.

Check for any sharp cut edge at the soil line and file or hide it. Line the inside with landscape fabric, set the bed in its final sunny spot, fill with soil, and plant. Total elapsed time should embarrass every other project you own.

images.alt: Raised garden planter with corrugated metal sides and cedar ends
